edge: The paper focuses on addressing the problem of efficient point searching and sampling for volume neural rendering.
Two typical approaches employed in volume neural rendering are rasterization (real-time with lower fidelity) and ray tracing (higher quality but longer rendering time).
Rasterization enables real-time rendering but sacrifices memory and fidelity.
Ray-tracing methods offer higher quality but have longer rendering times.

node_2: combination of rasterization and ray tracing
edge: HashPoint combines rasterization for efficient point search and ray marching for rendering
HashPoint rasterizes points within the camera's view for rapid searches.
HashPoint accelerates rendering by adaptive sampling on the primary surface encountered by the ray.
NeRF and its extensions use global MLPs for reconstructing radiance fields via ray marching
Global MLPs result in slow rendering times per scene.
HashPoint provides speedup for a range of ray-tracing-based methods without compromising accuracy.
HashPoint maintains equivalent or superior accuracy across both synthetic and real-world test datasets.

Ray marching leads to slow fitting due to extensive sampling of empty space.
Slow fitting results in increased reconstruction and rendering times.
